summary: Victorian English novelist who pioneered sensation fiction and detective fiction, best known for The Woman in White and The Moonstone. Close collaborator with Charles Dickens.

works.name: The Moonstone
works.description: Considered one of the first detective novels. The title diamond is stolen from Rachel Verinder at a birthday party, and Sergeant Cuff investigates. Godfrey Ablewhite is revealed as the thief. Franklin Blake unknowingly took the diamond while under the influence of laudanum and later recreates the events to reconstruct the crime. The gem was originally stolen during the Siege of Seringapatam. Three Brahmin 'jugglers' are sent to recover it. The novel ends with Rachel marrying Franklin and the diamond returned to an Indian statue. Narrators include the prim evangelist Drusilla Clack and butler Betteredge.

clues.clue: Godfrey Ablewhite stole the title diamond from Rachel Verinder
examples: Godfrey Ablewhite is revealed to be the one who stole the title diamond from Rachel Verinder

clues.clue: Sergeant Cuff investigates; obsessed with roses and gardening; employs 'Gooseberry'
examples: This geriatric policeman wins the friendship of the butler Betteredge and employs a boy with notably bulging eyes, Gooseberry

clues.clue: Franklin Blake unknowingly took the diamond under the influence of laudanum/opium; reenacts the events
examples: Franklin Blake recreates the events on a night when he was unknowingly given laudanum

clues.clue: Diamond originally stolen during Siege of Seringapatam; returned to India
examples: this novel's title object was initially stolen during the Siege of Seringapatam
This Collins work, considered to be the first detective novel

works.name: The Woman in White
works.description: A sensation novel in which art teacher Walter Hartright discovers Laura Fairlie resembles an asylum escapee named Anne Catherick. The obese Count Fosco and Sir Percival conspire against Laura. Marian Halcombe catches cold climbing on a roof in the rain to overhear their plot. Laura takes Anne's place in an asylum. Walter eventually restores Laura's identity by blackmailing Fosco. Fosco is murdered by an Italian 'Brotherhood.' The novel created a sensation fiction craze.

clues.clue: Walter Hartright discovers Laura Fairlie resembles asylum escapee Anne Catherick
examples: art teacher Walter Hartright discovers Laura Fairlie resembles an asylum escapee named Anne

clues.clue: Marian catches cold climbing to a roof in rain to overhear the plot against Laura
examples: a woman catches a cold after climbing to a roof in the pouring rain to overhear a plan to kill Laura Fairlie

clues.clue: Count Fosco is the obese villain; murdered by Italian 'Brotherhood'
examples: the obese Count Fosco betrays and is murdered by an Italian group called the 'Brotherhood'
created a mania for sensation novels by writing The Woman in White

works.name: Other Works and General
works.description: Collins had a laudanum addiction and believed he was accompanied by a doppelganger 'ghost.' He collaborated extensively with Charles Dickens, co-writing 'A House to Let' and The Frozen Deep (about the Franklin Expedition). His novel No Name features Magdalen Vanstone losing her inheritance. He also wrote Armadale and The New Magdalen. Sheridan Le Fanu was called 'the Irish Wilkie Collins.' Collins was a friend and traveling companion of Dickens.

clues.clue: Laudanum addiction; believed in a doppelganger 'ghost'
examples: believed that he was always accompanied by a doppelganger whom he called a 'ghost'

clues.clue: Collaborated with Dickens on 'A House to Let' and The Frozen Deep
examples: collaborated with his friend Charles Dickens in writing the story 'A House to Let'

clues.clue: No Name — Magdalen and Norah Vanstone lose inheritance

clues.clue: Close friendship with Dickens; traveling companions
examples: Dickens enjoyed a close friendship with this author; they were travelling buddies

comprehensive_summary:
Wilkie Collins (William Wilkie Collins) was a Victorian English novelist who pioneered both sensation fiction and detective fiction. He is most frequently clued for two novels: The Moonstone and The Woman in White.

The Moonstone is widely considered one of the first detective novels. The title diamond, originally stolen during the Siege of Seringapatam, is taken from Rachel Verinder at a birthday party. Sergeant Cuff, a gardening-obsessed detective who employs a boy nicknamed 'Gooseberry,' investigates. Godfrey Ablewhite is revealed as the thief, while Franklin Blake unknowingly took the diamond while under the influence of laudanum and later reenacts the events to prove his innocence. The novel features multiple narrators including the prim evangelist Drusilla Clack and the butler Betteredge. Three Brahmin 'jugglers' are sent to recover the gem. It ends with Rachel marrying Franklin and the diamond returned to an Indian statue.

The Woman in White created the sensation novel craze. Art teacher Walter Hartright discovers that Laura Fairlie resembles an asylum escapee named Anne Catherick. The obese Count Fosco and Sir Percival conspire against Laura. Marian Halcombe catches cold after climbing to a roof in the rain to overhear their plot. Laura is placed in an asylum in Anne's place. Walter restores Laura's identity by blackmailing Fosco, who is later murdered by an Italian 'Brotherhood.'

Collins had a laudanum addiction and believed he was accompanied by a doppelganger. He was a close friend and collaborator of Charles Dickens, co-writing 'A House to Let' and The Frozen Deep (about the Franklin Expedition). His other novels include No Name (about the Vanstone sisters losing their inheritance), Armadale, and The New Magdalen. Sheridan Le Fanu was called 'the Irish Wilkie Collins.'
